meta.name: SAPS Short Almost Perfect Scale
meta.description: The SAPS is an 8-item brief scale measuring two core dimensions of perfectionism: Standards (high personal standards) and Discrepancy (perceived gap between standards and performance), helping distinguish adaptive from maladaptive perfectionism.
meta.author: Rice, Richardson & Tueller (2014)
meta.reference: Rice SP, Richardson CME, Tueller S. The Short Form of the Revised Almost Perfect Scale. Journal of Personality Assessment. 2014;96(3):368-379.
meta.instruction: Please rate how much you agree with each statement.

questions.text: I have high standards for my performance at work or at school
I expect the best from myself
I often feel frustrated because I can't meet my goals
My best just never seems to be good enough for me
I rarely live up to my high standards
I am never satisfied with my accomplishments

ranges.label: Non-perfectionist
ranges.description: Your overall perfectionism score is low, suggesting that you do not place excessively high demands on yourself or experience significant distress about your performance. While some goal-setting can be beneficial, you may benefit from setting more structured personal standards.
suggestions: Consider whether setting slightly higher but achievable personal goals might help you grow and reach your potential more fully
Establishing specific, measurable objectives in areas that matter to you can provide motivation and a sense of accomplishment
Reflect on whether low standards reflect genuine contentment or possible disengagement from personal growth opportunities

ranges.label: Adaptive Perfectionist
ranges.description: Your score reflects adaptive perfectionism, characterized by high personal standards combined with a healthy ability to accept your performance. This balanced pattern is associated with achievement motivation, conscientiousness, and psychological well-being.
suggestions: Continue maintaining the healthy balance between striving for excellence and practicing self-acceptance when outcomes fall short
Stay vigilant for signs that your standards may be gradually becoming unrealistic or that self-criticism is increasing over time
Your adaptive perfectionism is a strength; channel it into meaningful goals while preserving flexibility and self-compassion

ranges.label: Maladaptive Perfectionist
ranges.description: Your score indicates maladaptive perfectionism, meaning you hold very high standards but frequently feel you cannot meet them. This pattern is associated with chronic self-criticism, anxiety, procrastination, and reduced satisfaction with accomplishments.
suggestions: Practice distinguishing between striving for excellence and demanding perfection; good enough is often genuinely sufficient
Develop self-compassion skills by treating yourself with the same kindness and understanding you would offer a friend
Focus on tracking your progress and effort rather than fixating on whether outcomes meet your ideal standards
Challenge all-or-nothing thinking patterns by recognizing that partial success and learning from mistakes are valuable outcomes
If perfectionism is significantly affecting your mental health or relationships, consider consulting a mental health professional

standards.label: Low Standards
standards.description: Your personal standards score is relatively low, indicating that you do not set particularly demanding expectations for yourself. While this may reduce performance-related stress, it could also limit your motivation to achieve your full potential.

standards.label: Moderate Standards
standards.description: Your personal standards are at a healthy moderate level. You set reasonable expectations for yourself that promote growth and achievement without creating excessive pressure or unrealistic demands on your performance.

standards.label: High Standards
standards.description: Your personal standards are very high, reflecting strong expectations for yourself across work and personal domains. High standards can drive achievement, but it is important to monitor whether they remain realistic and motivating rather than punishing.

discrepancy.label: Low Discrepancy
discrepancy.description: Your discrepancy score is low, indicating that you generally feel satisfied with your performance relative to your standards. This healthy pattern suggests you can accept imperfections and maintain self-esteem even when outcomes are not ideal.

discrepancy.label: Moderate Discrepancy
discrepancy.description: Your discrepancy score is moderate, suggesting that you sometimes feel your performance does not measure up to your expectations. Occasional self-criticism is normal, but persistent feelings of inadequacy may benefit from attention.

discrepancy.label: High Discrepancy
discrepancy.description: Your discrepancy score is high, indicating that you frequently perceive a painful gap between your standards and your actual performance. This chronic sense of falling short is strongly linked to anxiety, depression, and reduced life satisfaction.
